The underlying NDAA would authorize roughly $1.15 trillion for national defense, including pay raises ranging from 5% to 7% for service members, expanded weapons and munitions production and new investments in missile defense, autonomous systems, hypersonics and other emerging technologies.

The bill would also formally change the Department of Defense’s name to the Department of War, deeming references to the defense secretary and department in federal law to mean the secretary and Department of War.

The NDAA sets defense policy and authorizes spending levels but does not provide the money itself. Congress must separately pass defense appropriations legislation to fund the programs and priorities included in the measure.

Consideration of the legislation had been delayed for three weeks by a separate Republican fight over the SAVE America Act, Trump-backed election legislation requiring documentary proof of citizenship to register to vote and identification to cast a ballot in federal elections.

The House approved the election measure in February, but it stalled in the Senate, where Republicans lacked the 60 votes needed to overcome a filibuster.

Republican leaders revived the legislation through a procedure directing the House clerk to combine it with the NDAA after passage but before sending the package to the Senate. That approach allowed the House to vote on the defense and election measures separately before transmitting them as one bill.

The plan initially collapsed June 30 when 13 Republicans joined Democrats in voting against the procedural rule. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, R-Fla., and other holdouts later relented after Speaker Mike Johnson promised to attach the election legislation to appropriations bills and other must-pass measures.

The combined defense and election package faces an uncertain future in the Senate, where Democrats blocked consideration of their chamber’s NDAA last week in protest of the Trump administration’s war with Iran.

The debate also comes as the Pentagon seeks additional money to cover the conflict and replenish U.S. weapons stockpiles. The White House has requested an $87.6 billion supplemental package, including $67.1 billion for defense needs, while House Republicans are considering additional military and intelligence funding through a party-line reconciliation package.

item.title: AOC surges past Buttigieg, leaves Harris in the dust in key 2028 battleground poll
item.description: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez surged to 22% in the New Hampshire poll, edging Pete Buttigieg by one point as the 2028 Democratic primary field takes shape.
item.content:encoded:
Progressive firebrand Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez has surged to the front of the prospective 2028 Democratic presidential field in New Hampshire, edging former Transportation Secretary Pete Buttigieg and leaving former Vice President Kamala Harris far behind in a new poll.

Ocasio-Cortez drew 22% support among likely Democratic primary voters in the University of New Hampshire Granite State Poll released Wednesday, one point ahead of Buttigieg and 17 points ahead of Harris.

Ocasio-Cortez gained seven points since the February survey, while Harris and California Gov. Gavin Newsom lost ground — another sign of the four-term New York lawmaker’s growing strength among Democratic voters in the early presidential battleground and of her overall burgeoning popularity and standing in the party.

MIDTERM BATTLE FOR THE HOUSE: CHECK OUT THE FOX NEWS POWER RANKINGS

Ocasio-Cortez has been coy about her 2028 plans and careful not to rule out either a White House run in the race to succeed term-limited President Donald Trump, or a bid for the Senate against longtime Democratic Leader Sen. Chuck Schumer, who is up for re-election in two years. When asked about a run for higher office, Ocasio-Cortez recently said her goal is to "change the country."

Buttigieg, a moderate who as the former mayor of South Bend, Indiana, came in second in the 2020 New Hampshire presidential primary, has crisscrossed the country in support of fellow Democrats the past year as speculation builds around a possible 2028 run.

"Nearly half of socialists and one-third of progressives support Ocasio-Cortez, while pluralities of liberals and moderates support Buttigieg," Andrew Smith, the director of the University of New Hampshire Survey Center, said.

KAMALA HARRIS' TRAVELS AND COMMENTS CLEARLY POINT TO A 2028 RUN

Sen. Mark Kelly of Arizona, a retired astronaut, stood in third place in the poll, at 9%, with the term-limited Newsom a point back. The California governor's support dropped seven points since the February survey.

Independent Sen. Bernie Sanders of Vermont, the 2016 and 2020 runner-up in the Democratic presidential primaries, stood at 6%. The 84-year-old Sanders, asked about another White House run, said last month, "I suspect that's not going to happen." He jokingly added, "I know I look like I'm 30. I am not."

Meanwhile, Harris, the party's 2024 nominee after she replaced then-President Joe Biden, who dropped out less than four months before the election following a disastrous debate against Trump, grabbed only 5% support in the new poll, down from 10% in February.

While the former vice president's numbers in the latest New Hampshire survey are nothing to brag about, she tops an average of the most recent national polls in the hypothetical race for the 2028 Democratic nomination. Surveys in the early stages of a presidential cycle are often heavily influenced by the name recognition of the potential candidates.

DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB

Everyone else in the large field of potential contenders tested in the New Hampshire poll registered at 2% or less.

New Hampshire held the first-in-the-nation presidential primary for a century, before Democrats upended their nominating calendar in the 2024 cycle, moving South Carolina to the lead-off position. The Democratic National Committee's (DNC) Rules and Bylaws panel is likely on Friday to unveil a new calendar for the 2028 cycle, which would be voted on at the DNC's summer meeting in Austin, Texas, next month.

Republicans have not altered their calendar, keeping the Iowa caucuses as their lead-off state, followed by New Hampshire.

In the race for the GOP presidential nomination, 36% of likely Republican primary voters in New Hampshire said they would support Vice President JD Vance, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio at 26%.

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

Sen. Rand Paul of Kentucky, who ran for the nomination in 2016 and is mulling another bid, stood at 8% in the survey, along with former ambassador and former South Carolina Gov. Nikki Haley, who was the runner-up to Trump in 2024.

Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis, who also bid for the nomination in 2024, grabbed 6% support, with everyone else tested at 4% or less.

item.title: Pete Golding drops unfiltered truth on 'Ex' Lane Kiffin, and NCAA tampering: 'What have we created?'
item.description: Pete Golding addressed his relationship with Lane Kiffin at SEC Media Days, revealing he even bought one of Kiffin's Oxford houses recently.
item.content:encoded:
TAMPA, FL - Pete Golding took the stage on Wednesday at SEC Media Days and was clearly prepared to give his opinion about the current state of college football, along with the obvious questions about Lane Kiffin.

If you know the Ole Miss head coach, none of this should come as a surprise, given his lack of filter regarding life, or just about anything else.

Florida coach brings laughs, no filter and Lane Kiffin comparison rebuttal to first SEC media days

Obviously, one of the main questions some in the room were prepared to quiz Golding on was his relationship with former Ole Miss head coach Lane Kiffin. But unless you've been sleeping under a rock for the past few months, his feelings toward his former boss are pretty well known.

Yes, they are still friends, but that doesn’t mean they agree on everything, which would include Kiffin's exit for LSU. And yes, they still communicate on what is seemingly a daily basis, though Golding does go a few days without responding to Kiffin if he's in the mood to mess with him just a bit.

But, the story regarding the Ole Miss versus LSU showdown in Oxford this coming season is more geared towards the players and fans of each school. For the coaches, sure they'd thoroughly enjoy beating each other on the field, but it's not as if either of them came out in a rough spot following the chaos last December.

LANE KIFFIN CAN'T STOP TALKING ABOUT OLE MISS, EVEN WHILE EXPLAINING WHY HE CHOSE LSU OVER FLORIDA

"I get this everywhere we go...Lane and I are good," Golding mentioned. "I did not know coach Kiffin prior to taking the Ole Miss job. I think everybody just assumes Alabama, Alabama (assistant roles) when coach Kiffin was (there). I didn’t come to Ole Miss because of Kiffin, I came to Ole Miss to raise three kids. Our relationship grew over those three years.

"I'm very appreciative of how he treated me and my family, and learned a lot from him. I have no ill-will towards Coach Kiffin. He texts me on a regular basis, and there are certain things we'll talk about, and certain things we don't talk about. He's very competitive like I'm very competitive.."

While there is still a lot of obvious frustration between the Ole Miss athletic department and Lane Kiffin, things seemed to work out just fine for Pete Golding.

Sure, I'd imagine he did not want to be thrust into the spotlight that late in the 2025 season, right before the playoff was set to begin. But, Pete Golding was also hellbent on staying in Oxford, no matter if it was him being given the keys to Ole Miss, or staying on as an assistant coach.

Heck, Golding actually ended up buying one of Lane Kiffin's houses in the Oxford area over the past six months.

"There's always going to be history, it's the ex, right?," Golding joked about Kiffin.

On Wednesday, as the questions continued to be asked about the relationship between Kiffin and Golding, the Rebels coach had a different mindset on how some of the issues played out to end the 2025 regular season.

"I tell a lot of people this. I probably shouldn’t, but I don't care: In 2021, if you said you can look in 2025 and you're about to be 11-1 and go to the playoff. Would you take it if you're coach was leaving? Hell yea, you would. Everybody won from it, it's ok. Every place is not for everybody.

"I'd be the first one to tell you I told him it would be dumb if he were to leave, I wanted him to stay. That was a selfish decision because I love being the DC where I was at. He decided it was better for him and his family. We play him like every other SEC team. We're gonna spot the ball and play."

When Dabo Swinney decided to go scorched-earth on Ole Miss for alleged tampering with LB Luke Fornelli, this opened up a can of worms that the Rebels head coach has now taken an interesting approach with.

Pete Golding makes it known he has tampering receipts, while also admitting Kiffin texts him way too much

Officials in Oxford have made it known to the NCAA that they are prepared to turn in other programs for tampering if the organization decides to impose penalties on Ole Miss for talking with Luke Fornelli while he was technically a part of the Clemson football team (for a week).

In terms of how Pete Golding feels, the Rebels coach had a pretty sensical answer when asked for his thoughts on tampering.

"You're a reporter right? Would you quit your job before interviewing for another job or being offered another job?," Golding asked rhetorically. "Right, nobody would. We have a portal you can go in with a 'Do Not Contact' tag. I'm gonna quit my job, where I'm getting paid and I'm gonna go in saying I need a new job, which nobody can call me to interview.

"Like, what have we created? So, we've created it along with representation for these players that it's their responsibility to get them paid well. By design, it's put a lot of people in bad positions."

While I don't think many people will argue with Pete Golding about the illogical things that the NCAA has created, I find it hard to imagine Dabo Swinney is going to agree.

But, in terms of acting as though colleges are acting above-board when it comes to recruiting, the Ole Miss head coach saved the most reasonable context of his rant for the end of his answer.

"Let's not act like recruiting in college football for the past 100 years is the most moral thing. What are we talking about?"

He's not wrong. Let's see how much the NCAA agrees.

item.title: France records more than 5,700 excess deaths during historic early summer heat wave
item.description: More than 5,700 excess deaths were recorded during France's unprecedented June heat wave, making it the country's deadliest since 2003, health officials said.
item.content:encoded:
France has recorded more than 5,700 excess deaths during its historic summer heat wave, marking the country's deadliest heat-related toll in more than two decades, Public Health France announced Wednesday.

Between June 17 and July 2, the agency expected 15,910 deaths but instead recorded 21,674. The difference — 5,764 excess deaths — was 36% higher than what would have been expected.

During the heat wave, temperatures climbed well above 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many parts of the country, Public Health France said.

"This unprecedented event, characterized by its early onset, intensity and duration, exposed almost the entire population to extreme heat during a period that was still largely dedicated to school and work activities," the agency said.

EUROPE'S FIRES REVEAL DEADLY MIX OF RECORD HEAT, HUMAN ERROR, ARSON AND LAND MISMANAGEMENT

Deaths from all causes increased across hospitals, private homes and nursing homes during the heat wave.

The agency's analysis included people ages 15 and older, with mortality increasing beginning at age 45. Adults 75 and older accounted for roughly two-thirds of the provisional death toll, representing at least 3,719 excess deaths.

SCIENTIST CALLS FOR AMERICANS TO CUT OFF AIR CONDITIONING IN SUMMER, CLAIMING IT CAUSES GLOBAL WARMING

The Paris region was hit hardest, recording 1,999 excess deaths, more than 80% above what would have been expected without the heat wave, according to the agency.

Public Health France said 2026 recorded the country's highest excess mortality linked to a heat wave since 2003. The National Institute of Health and Medical Research estimated that roughly 15,000 more people died compared to the usual mortality for that time of year.  

PARIS DEPUTY MAYOR BLAMES UNITED STATES' CARBON EMISSIONS FOR DEADLY HEAT WAVE

This year, the hottest days occurred on June 24 and June 25, when the national average temperature reached 86 degrees Fahrenheit. Peak temperatures also climbed above 104 degrees in many areas.

More than half of the excess deaths — about 56% — occurred during or immediately after the most intense stretch of the heat wave between June 25 and June 27.

Officials also said more than 10,000 excess deaths were reported across Europe during the peak of the heat wave, though the preliminary figures are expected to be updated in the coming weeks.

According to The Associated Press, funeral directors in Paris said they were inundated by the surge in bodies awaiting burial or cremation, with some mortuaries reaching capacity and turning down new arrivals.

Health officials noted that the excess mortality estimate includes deaths from all causes that occurred during the heat wave. The figures remain provisional and will continue to be refined in the coming weeks.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

item.title: Hasan Piker draws fresh scrutiny after framing newspaper covering 9/11 attacks in stream background
item.description: Hasan Piker faced renewed scrutiny after viewers spotted what appeared to be a framed newspaper front page reporting the 9/11 attacks in his Twitch stream background.
item.content:encoded:
Controversial streamer Hasan Piker is facing renewed scrutiny over a recent addition to his visual background during his streams. Viewers noticed what appeared to be a framed copy of the front page of a newspaper with an image of the Sept. 11, 2001, attack on the Twin Towers in New York City.

The earliest livestream reviewed by Fox News Digital showing the newspaper in the background was broadcast on July 13. The front page appears to be The New York Times' Sept. 12, 2001, edition, which was headlined, "U.S. Attacked: Hijacked Jets Destroy Twin Towers and Hit Pentagon in Day of Terror." The X account DSA Watch on Tuesday posted a clip of Piker's stream, where he discussed the newspaper front page framed on the wall behind him.

"You might have noticed something, some changes behind me. I finally framed the cutie Cinderella newspaper from 9/11, the morning of 9/12… the newspaper that covered 9/11. It's now officially sitting behind me," he told viewers earlier this month.

Piker was widely criticized after saying that "America deserved 9/11" during a stream in 2019. After facing immense backlash, Piker said in an interview that he "obviously" did not mean that America deserved the attacks and accused critics of disregarding "the actual truth of what I was talking about," explaining the viral quote as a critique of U.S. foreign policy.

ABDUL EL-SAYED SAYS RALLYING WITH HASAN PIKER DOESN'T MEAN HE ENDORSES 'AMERICA DESERVED 9/11' REMARK

"I should have used more precise and better use of language there," Piker said in the interview. "It's messed up that I would give the opportunity to the right to try to morally grandstand on an issue like this when they are 100% responsible for all the bloodshed that has been caused."

He later admitted in the interview that his comments on 9/11 were "inappropriate."

The renewed attention comes as Piker's influence spreads beyond his hours-long streams.

Piker was featured as a speaker at the College Democrats of America's (CDA) convention, held in Washington, D.C., from July 17 through July 19. Other speakers included Leaders We Deserve founder David Hogg, American Federation of Teachers President Randi Weingarten, as well as Democratic lawmakers and political influencers, according to CDA's Instagram posts. However, none drew as much scrutiny as Piker.

The streamer was mocked for wearing what many described as a Mao Zedong-esque jacket while going off on "Democrats who compromise, who conciliate, and who betray us."

"No more to the fascist reactionaries, but no more to the Democrats who will collaborate and cooperate with the fascists, who will fund ICE, who will fund Israel, who will help poison the planet, who will stiff workers and help bosses," Piker said.

WISCONSIN DEMOCRAT DOWNPLAYS APPEARING ON HASAN PIKER'S SHOW, DOESN'T 'ENDORSE' EVERYTHING HE SAYS

One of the co-sponsors for the event, J Street U, the campus arm of left-leaning J Street, which aims to be the "political home and voice for pro-Israel, pro-peace, pro-democracy Americans," faced backlash for its affiliation. Jeremy Ben Ami, the organization's founder, pushed back on the criticism in a post on X.

"J Street U was proud to sponsor and participate in the College Democrats Convention. We had no role in the selection of speakers, including the decision to have Hasan Piker as a surprise guest, with whom we have many disagreements," Ben Ami told Fox News Digital.

"Being in venues like this is even more important when there are participants with whom we disagree. Failing to show up would have been an abdication of our responsibility to make the case for our pro-Israel views," he added.

Ben Ami did not clarify where J Street's views diverge from Piker's nor where they align.

DSA Watch was one of the groups that criticized Piker's CDA convention speech, and the Twitch streamer hit back.

While addressing the criticisms of his speech in a stream, Piker made several claims about DSA Watch, including saying that it was "a right-wing operation" and "most likely a hasbara operation as well," meaning an Israeli operation. DSA Watch denied the claims and said Piker's "source" was related to "a failed dox attempt."

The CDA Jewish Caucus also objected to Piker's inclusion in the convention, calling him "antisemitic and misogynistic."

"The normalization of Piker and his rhetoric in CDA-sanctioned programming is a direct threat to Jewish safety and inclusion within the College Democrats community. Applying a double standard to the safety and representation of Jewish students is a manifestation of antisemitism and is inconsistent with our shared Democratic values of inclusion, respect for diverse communities, and concern for the safety and belonging of all members," a statement from the caucus read.

FAR-LEFT STREAMER HASAN PIKER DEFENDS HIMSELF FROM ANTISEMITISM ACCUSATIONS IN INTERVIEW WITH JEWISH OUTLET

Following the release of the CDA Jewish Caucus' statement, the Anti-Defamation League weighed in, expressing its disappointment that CDA chose to have Piker at its convention.

"It's deeply concerning to see the next generation of political leaders help normalize antisemitism rather than reject it," the ADL wrote on X.

The CDA convention speech and the framed Sept. 12, 2001, newspaper have added new layers to the ongoing debate over Piker's inclusion in Democratic political circles.

Some Democrats, including Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed, State Assemblywoman Claire Valdez, D-N.Y., and activist Darializa Avila Chevalier, have campaigned with Piker. The campaign appearances sparked debates among Democrats over whether embracing Piker would help them reach younger voters or if he was worth the heat given his rhetoric.

Fox News Digital reached out to Piker and CDA for comment.

item.title: Trump-appointed judge torpedoes DOJ lawsuit against Minnesota's sanctuary immigration rules
item.description: A Trump-appointed judge ruled Minnesota cannot be compelled to assist ICE, dismissing every claim in the administration's sanctuary cities lawsuit.
item.content:encoded:
A Trump-appointed judge dealt the Trump administration a major setback Monday, tossing out its lawsuit against Minnesota's sanctuary immigration policies and ruling the state cannot be compelled to assist federal immigration enforcement.

The battle centered on whether the Trump administration can use the Supremacy Clause to knock down Minnesota's sanctuary laws, which include policies that bar local officials from holding immigrants solely on ICE detainers, restrict participation in federal immigration enforcement and limit the sharing of driver's license and immigration-related data with ICE.

The Justice Department argued Minnesota's restrictions illegally hinder ICE, but Chief U.S. District Judge Eric Tostrud rejected that argument, ruling states have the constitutional authority to decide how their own officials participate in immigration enforcement.

In a 56-page opinion, Tostrud dismissed every claim brought by the Justice Department against Minnesota, Minneapolis, St. Paul and Hennepin County, concluding the challenged laws regulate only state and local officials, not the federal government.

DOJ ESCALATES BLUE-STATE ICE STANDOFF AFTER STATES REFUSE KEY FEDERAL REQUEST

"They do not command the federal government or its contractors to act. They do not forbid the federal government or its contractors from acting. They do not control how the federal government or its contractors undertake their work," Tostrud wrote. "Rather, they regulate how state and local officials perform their duties."

The ruling marks a significant setback for the administration's broader effort to block sanctuary policies across the country by arguing they unlawfully interfere with federal immigration enforcement.

But Eric Wessan, an attorney in the Iowa Solicitor General's Office, said he believed Tostrud's ruling conflicts with an earlier Eighth Circuit decision that he says gives the federal government stronger grounds to challenge state laws that interfere with federal immigration enforcement.

"States across the country have passed laws making state immigration crimes. The Eighth Circuit (which includes Minnesota) found one of those laws obstructed federal immigration enforcement, and so it was preempted," Wessan told Fox News Digital. "Judge Tostrud cited that opinion to explain that preemption is narrow. I think that his opinion is hard to square with the binding precedent."

FIRST ON FOX: DOJ SUES SPANBERGER’S VIRGINIA OVER LAWS KNEECAPPING FEDERAL AGENTS AS MASK WAR ESCALATES

The Justice Department argued Minnesota's laws and local ordinances conflict with federal immigration statutes by restricting local officers from honoring ICE detainers, limiting information sharing with federal authorities and preventing local officials from participating in immigration enforcement.

But Tostrud concluded that accepting the administration's claim would create a constitutional problem under the Tenth Amendment's anti-commandeering doctrine.

The judge emphasized that while Congress possesses broad authority over immigration, it cannot compel states to use their own personnel and resources to carry out federal regulatory programs.

"The framers explicitly chose a Constitution that confers upon Congress the power to regulate individuals, not States," the opinion states, adding that the federal government "may not command the States' officers...to administer or enforce a federal regulatory program."

NEW YORK SHERIFFS 'MAD AS HELL' AS HOCHUL PUSHES TO BAN KEY LAW ENFORCEMENT PARTNERSHIP

Wessan agreed that states generally cannot be required to assist the federal government but argued the harder legal question is whether states can prohibit voluntary cooperation.

"States are not required to help the federal government enforce federal immigration law," he said. "Whether States can ban cooperation when certain actors (states or municipalities want to) is a harder question, and probably depends on the specific state and state laws."

Tostrud also rejected one of the administration's central arguments — that Minnesota's refusal to cooperate with ICE amounts to obstruction of federal immigration enforcement.

"Trefusing to help is not the same as impeding," Tostrud wrote.

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

Wessan argued the debate over that distinction distracts from the larger immigration crisis.

"The country is facing an immigration crisis," he said. "Rather than figuring out the fine line between impeding and refusing to help, Minnesota should appreciate the federal government's willingness to help with the illegal immigration crisis—and the waste, fraud, and abuse that accompanies it."

Minnesota leaders have defended the state's sanctuary-style policies, arguing they encourage immigrants in the country illegally to cooperate with local law enforcement by assuring them that reporting crimes or serving as witnesses will not automatically expose them to immigration enforcement.

The legal dispute also follows months of heightened immigration enforcement in Minnesota, where the Trump administration launched Operation Metro Surge, a large-scale ICE initiative that drew national attention, sparked protests and significantly increased the federal law enforcement presence across the state.

The operation intensified after two U.S. citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, were shot and killed by federal agents just weeks apart in January. Their deaths heightened scrutiny of Minnesota's immigration policies and prompted President Donald Trump to remove then-Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem from overseeing the operation, with border czar Tom Homan taking over the effort.

After taking over, Homan repeatedly argued that large-scale ICE operations would become less necessary if state and local officials cooperated with federal immigration authorities. Homan said that his "main focus now is drawdown," but stressed that reducing the federal presence would require greater cooperation from Minnesota officials.

"Cooperation with local authorities, especially jails and prisons, will give federal agents better access to people in the United States illegally. This would then lead to fewer federal law enforcement [agents] on Minnesota streets," Homan said.

Monday's ruling comes as the Trump administration continues to pursue similar lawsuits challenging sanctuary policies in several other states.

Fox News Digital reached out to the White House and the Justice Department for comment.

item.title: Christian Pulisic recovers from his World Cup injury by hanging out with Megan Moroney in Nashville
item.description: Christian Pulisic was spotted with country singer Megan Moroney at Dierks Bentley's Whiskey Row in Nashville, sparking relationship speculation.
item.content:encoded:
Christian Pulisic and the USMNT were bounced from the World Cup in the Round of 16 by Belgium back on July 6. He left what ended up being a 4-1 loss early with an apparent ankle injury.

What can be gathered from his recent sightings is that he's enjoying the time off he was looking forward to when asked about the injury after the loss. The soccer world didn’t appreciate him taking himself out early.

Pulisic doesn't seem to be too concerned about that. He's moving on from the World Cup. He's got country music concerts to attend and at least one country music star to hang out with in Nashville.

LANDON DONOVAN JOINS CHRISTIAN PULISIC CRITICISM FOLLOWING USA'S WORLD CUP EXIT WITH HARSH OBSERVATION

In other words, the injury is doing much better now. Thanks for asking.

Last week, Christian took his cousin to a Megan Moroney concert in Florida. On Monday night, he was spotted hanging out in Nashville with the singer at Dierks Bentley's Whiskey Row on Broadway.

Naturally, a wounded soccer star catching up on his rest and a big-name country music star being spotted out together has people talking and speculating about the nature of their relationship.

LA GALAXY COACH GREG VANNEY SAYS TEAM USA WAS 'TOO NAIVE,' EXPLAINS CHRISTIAN PULISIC'S STRUGGLES

Are these two friends? Or is there something more going on here? Is she helping to nurse him back to full strength through the power of song and companionship?

The answers to those questions are currently unknown. Here's what is known. Pulisic and Moroney follow each other on Instagram, which is always a fun game to play.

They've also been seen together a couple of times now on social media and Pulisic is having a better summer than a lot of people are.

Good for him.

This relationship, if that's what it is, will do much more for the sport of soccer in the U.S. than him playing injured in a loss that at that point was going to happen whether he was on the field or not.

item.title: The overlooked way the Iran war is making groceries, Amazon packages and new homes more expensive
item.description: Diesel prices surged from $3.56 to $5.13 per gallon after the Iran conflict, threatening to raise the prices of groceries and everyday essentials.
item.content:encoded:
The fuel most Americans never think about could become the Iran conflict's biggest economic consequence, driving up the cost of transporting nearly everything Americans buy. Diesel powers the nation's trucks, farms and freight network, making it one of the economy's most important, yet overlooked, drivers of inflation.

Unlike gasoline, which primarily affects motorists filling up their tanks, diesel is the workhorse fuel behind the nation's supply chain. From the tractors harvesting crops and the trucks hauling groceries to supermarket shelves to the heavy equipment building new homes and the Amazon package landing on your doorstep, chances are diesel helped move it there.

As diesel prices climb, businesses face higher transportation costs that economists say often ripple through the economy, raising the price of everyday essentials from groceries and consumer goods to construction materials and adding new pressure to already strained household budgets.

'KEEP A LOW PROFILE': STATE DEPARTMENT WARNS AMERICANS OVERSEAS THEY COULD BE TARGETED

Those economic ripple effects could extend into the political arena.

With both parties expected to make affordability a centerpiece of their 2026 midterm campaigns, a prolonged rise in diesel prices could make it harder to persuade voters that the cost of living is moving in the right direction.

The latest surge in diesel prices underscores how geopolitical conflicts can reach American households in unexpected ways.

While drivers may notice higher gasoline prices first as driving budgets are affected, economists say diesel often has a broader and longer-lasting impact because it influences the cost of moving goods and affects nearly every product that arrives at a store. Economists say the fuel deserves closer attention during oil shocks because it serves as the backbone of the nation's freight network, making diesel a key barometer of whether geopolitical turmoil will remain confined to energy markets or spread throughout the broader economy.

Diesel prices, which averaged $3.56 a gallon in January 2025, have climbed to $5.13 after a sharp run-up following the Iran conflict, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

A fully loaded semitruck typically gets just 6 to 7 miles per gallon of diesel, according Department of Energy data. Filling its roughly 250-gallon tanks can cost more than $1,280 at today's prices. For fleets logging more than 100,000 miles a year, even modest increases in diesel prices can add thousands of dollars in annual operating costs.

"We all focus on gasoline because, ultimately, we're consumers and pump prices are very visible. But what we don't think about is the price of diesel, which is the workhorse fuel for the U.S. economy and especially for key sectors," Bernard Yaros, lead U.S. economist for Oxford Economics, told Fox News Digital.

Yaros said the recent rise in diesel prices is especially concerning because of its potential impact on food inflation. Diesel fuels nearly every stage of the agricultural supply chain, from farm equipment to the trucks that deliver groceries, meaning higher fuel costs can eventually work their way onto supermarket shelves.

"From an inflationary perspective, I'm very concerned about the recent rise in diesel prices as it pertains to the cost of food or grocery store prices," Yaros said. 

"Take the food industry, for instance. Diesel powers the irrigation pumps, the tractors in the field and the trucks that bring food from the farm to your local grocery store. It's part of every layer of food production in the U.S."

BBQ LOVERS BEWARE: MIDDLE EAST CONFLICT MIGHT DISRUPT YOUR SUMMER PLANS THIS YEAR

An energy industry source, who requested anonymity due to not being authorized to speak publicly, said diesel has become one of the clearest examples of how geopolitical shocks can ripple through global energy markets.

"The great majority of the price movement that you've seen in diesel markets over the last five months has been the direct result of the conflict in Iran and specifically the closure of the Strait of Hormuz," the source told Fox News Digital.

Few places matter more to global energy markets than the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway between Iran, Oman and the United Arab Emirates.

Roughly 20 million barrels of oil pass through the strait each day, along with about one-fifth of the world's liquefied natural gas. The waterway is also a critical export route for refined fuels such as diesel, gasoline and jet fuel, meaning even limited disruptions can tighten global supplies, jolt energy markets and push fuel costs higher around the world.

WHY THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ MATTERS AS TRUMP ISSUES FRESH ULTIMATUM TO IRAN

Even if tensions in the Middle East ease, analysts say diesel prices may not quickly return to pre-conflict levels.

"Refineries don't process crude instantaneously," the industry source said. "A lot of times what you're filling up your car with today was refined a week and a half ago and was produced two months before that."

Because crude must be transported, refined and distributed before it reaches consumers, higher diesel costs can continue rippling through the economy even after oil markets begin to stabilize. The result is that today's diesel spike could linger long after the headlines about the Middle East fade, quietly making its way through supply chains and, eventually, onto grocery store shelves.

item.title: Gold medal-winning Team USA hockey players will be making an appearance on 'Celebrity Family Feud'
item.description: Team USA men featuring Connor Hellebuyck and Dylan Larkin face the women's squad led by Hilary Knight on "Celebrity Family Feud" on Thursday.
item.content:encoded:
The year 2026 is just about halfway over, but it is already one of the greatest on record for USA Hockey.

This year, the Americans swept the gold medals at the Winter Olympics in Milan and Cortina, with the men's team, the women's team, and the Paralympic team all taking the top step of the podium in their respective tournaments.

And that's good enough for one of the highest honors our nation has to offer: an appearance on "Celebrity Family Feud."

Oh, man. The hallowed hall that Richard Dawson, Louie Anderson, Richard Karn, John O'Hurley, and Steve Harvey built.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE OUTKICK SPORTS COVERAGE

The episode will air on Thursday at 8 p.m. ET on ABC, and will feature Team USA men — comprised of NHLers Dylan Larkin and Connor Hellebuyck, along with Paralympians Griffin LaMarre, Jack Wallace, and Declan Farmer — against Team USA women, with captain Hilary Knight, Hannah Bilka, Cayla Barnes, Laila Edwards, and Caroline Harvey representing the team.

I'm all in on this. I admittedly haven't watched "Family Feud" — celebrity or civilian edition — in quite some time, but it's an institution. I'd go so far as to say it's one of our nation's great game shows. Up there with "The Price Is Right," "Hollywood Squares," and that one Steph Curry came up with that was basically just people playing minigolf on the same obstacle course from the show "Wipeout."

STANLEY CUP FINAL RATINGS SHOW OLYMPIC HOCKEY FEVER IS ALIVE AND WELL IN THE UNITED STATES

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

Hell, I might tune in just to watch Steve Harvey wrestle with the name "Connor Hellebuyck." That alone sounds like gold, Jerry.

It's also a great opportunity to get some Team USA faces back on TV as the entire USA Hockey program from top to bottom has never been stronger.

The next World Cup of Hockey isn't until 2028, and the next Olympics aren't until 2030. So whatever can keep American puck — and hockey in general — in the bloodstream as it continues this heater it's been on since the 4 Nations Face-Off is always going to be a great idea.

item.title: Dem gov lashes out as Trump HUD scrutinizes nearly $2B in disaster funds over fraud concerns
item.description: HUD suspended disaster aid procurements over fraud and abuse concerns tied to nearly $2 billion, drawing accusations of political motives from Virgin Islands Gov. Albert Bryan Jr.
item.content:encoded:
Virgin Islands Gov. Albert Bryan Jr. slammed the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) after the agency suspended procurements involving the territory's housing finance authority over concerns about fraud and abuse tied to nearly $2 billion in disaster-recovery aid.

Bryan, a Democrat, said HUD’s suspension came as a "shock" and called HUD's letter "unprofessional." He claimed the U.S. Virgin Islands' disaster-recovery issues have been "sensationalized" and that the suspension is an "overreach."

"When I go to Washington, I don't go and visit [HUD or FEMA] anymore because we don't have any problems," Bryan claimed when pressed by local media at a public briefing in Charlotte Amalie.

MINNESOTA OF THE CARIBBEAN? TRUMP ADMIN FREEZES VACATION HOTSPOT’S FUNDS OVER ALLEGED HURRICANE-AID ABUSES

At one point, Bryan suggested HUD’s announcement could be a "political stunt" timed with Tuesday's start of early voting in the territory’s contested gubernatorial election. Bryan is term-limited.

"[W]hy does the press have it and why does Fox News in particular have it and why are we only hearing about this now?" Bryan fumed – as Fox News Digital exclusively broke the news Tuesday.

"We'll be appealing [HUD’s suspension] and then get into the bottom of what exactly is generating this kind of harsh motives in the Virgin Islands."

TRUMP ADMIN PAUSING OVER $1B IN MEDICAID PAYMENTS TO MINNESOTA, CALIFORNIA OVER FRAUD, COMPLIANCE CONCERNS

Fox News Digital reached out to HUD for comment.

When Bryan said an audit cited in HUD's suspension notice had ended some time ago, a reporter noted a separate HUD report found the Virgin Islands Housing Finance Authority's risk management practices remained "below the lowest desired level" as recently as March 2025.

The reporter asked Bryan whether it was fair to say the territorial government had been "blindsided" by HUD's letter and subsequent news reports.

VANCE TURNS UP HEAT ON STATES WITH FEDERAL CASH THREAT OVER MEDICAID FRAUD CRACKDOWN

"Absolutely," Bryan replied. "[E]ven if it was up to 2025 it covers a period where they have already addressed it in fact the $6.2 million in-question cost was addressed years ago."

Bryan said he "perused" the letter from Andrew Hughes, a deputy to HUD Secretary Scott Turner, and argued the territory has made significant progress on several recovery projects, including one at Magen's Bay, a world-famous calm-water beach on St. Thomas.

He suggested some of the issues with recovery projects in the U.S. Virgin Islands have to do with costs, saying one public housing unit can expend $1 million – "which is crazy."

"We’ve had to send out bids multiple times because the costs are not aligning with what the national standards are," he said.

In a separate statement, Bryan said many of the matters described in HUD’s letter "reflect issues that have been known for some time."

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

"According to the Virgin Islands Housing Finance Authority, significant corrective actions have already been undertaken in the years since many of those findings were first identified."

"It is important that this process be handled in a manner that protects the people of the Virgin Islands and does not unnecessarily disrupt critical housing and disaster-recovery programs," he said.

Bryan said at his press conference that his administration previously addressed "weaknesses" at VIHFA earlier in his administration.

"We have done a lot of work in order to make sure that the HFA had the capacity – if you can remember the first three years of the administration, we talked about capacity planning... which has slowed the recovery considerably from their view."

HUD's suspension comes as Turner plays a leading role on the White House Task Force to Eliminate Fraud, as the Trump administration continues its wide-scale effort across agencies to curb abuse of taxpayer funds.

Fox News Digital reached out to Bryan’s office for additional comment.
